docs.title: AuthC v0.1 — Authority-First Specification for AuthN and AuthZ
docs.text: Consent Based Digital-ID Protocol April 3, 2026

docs.title: Draft IPR + participation
docs.text: This initial AuthC v0.1 draft is published under 0PN RF-RAND and governed by the OPN Lab General Participation Agreement (GPA) for work aligned to the ISO/IEC 27560-1 profile.

docs.title: Table of Contents (draft)
docs.text:
1. Document control 0.1 Title, version, status, date 0.2 Editors, contributors, review group 0.3 Normative language (MUST/SHOULD/MAY) 0.4 Change log + release policy (consensus-governed)
  2. Introduction 1.1 Purpose (authority-first operational internet privacy) 1.2 Scope (digital identification management; controller practice transparency) 1.3 Non-goals / exclusions (v0.1 boundary) 1.4 Audience and intended use
  3. Core principles (normative) 2.1 Transparency-by-Default (TbD) ordering rule 2.2 Authority-first rule (scope of authority for surveillance at all times) 2.3 Macro-data-only raw transfer rule (open internet constraint) 2.4 Separation: authority/consent vs permission (human vs system act) 2.5 Evidence is a security property (no post-hoc laundering)
  4. Terms, roles, and concepts
  5. Normative artifacts (what exists, always)
  6. Data classification + transfer rules (normative)
  7. Convention 108+ code-of-practice mapping (normative)
  8. AuthC 1.2.2 — Order of Operations (Operational Internet Privacy) (normative)
  9. AuthC Exchange (authority protocol) (normative)
  10. Conformance (normative)
  11. Governance: spec evolution by consensus (normative)
  12. Security and failure modes (informative)

Appendix A: Minimal field templates Appendix B: Worked examples Appendix C: Crosswalk to existing v1.x exchange docs

docs.title: 1.1 Purpose
docs.text:
AuthC is a form of digital identification management for digital public infrastructure, developed with and for the ISO/IEC 27560-1 profile for notice and consent receipt systems.

AuthC governs authentication (authN) and authorization (authZ) by requiring authority-first disclosure, evidence-grade receipts, and enforceable ordering before any identity verification, identifier binding, or permission enforcement.

AuthC specifies an authority-first, Transparency-by-Default code of practice for Convention 108+ in digital identification management contexts.

The objective is to make controller practice inspectable before any surveillance-relevant act occurs, by requiring controller identification, declared scope of authority for surveillance, and evidence-grade receipt history.

docs.title: 1.2 Scope
docs.text: This specification covers: - Digital identification management as a surveillance-relevant practice (identifier binding + metadata creation). - The rule that only macro authority data (controller identification + authority scope disclosure) may travel as raw/open data across the internet. - Authority evolution through consensus, captured in AuthC Exchange outcomes and receipt history.

docs.title: 1.3 Non-goals
docs.text:
• Transport/API protocol details (left to implementation profiles)
  • Detailed cryptographic schemes (left to higher-assurance profiles)
  • Certification role mechanics (left to later versions)

docs.title: 2.1 Controller-ID first
docs.text: Controller identification MUST be disclosed and retrievable before any identifier binding, tracking, inference, or collection.

docs.title: 2.2 Authority-first
docs.text: Scope of authority for surveillance MUST be disclosed (versioned) and bound to receipts so authority-in-force can be reconstructed at time T.

docs.title: 2.3 Macro-data-only raw transfer
docs.text:
Only macro authority data may be transferred as raw/open data across the internet.

Any micro-context data or individual-linked data MUST be authority-scoped, evidence-linked, and proportionate.

docs.title: 2.4 Consent (authority) vs permission (enforcement)
docs.text:
Consent (when used) is the human authority act and/or legal-basis condition.

Permission is system enforcement downstream of disclosed authority and proof-of-notice.

docs.title: ISO/IEC TS 27560:2023 (Base Standard)
docs.text: Privacy technologies — Consent record information structure

docs.title: Start here
docs.text: • ISO catalogue entry (public): https://www.iso.org/standard/80392.html

docs.title: What this standard provides
docs.text: ISO/IEC TS 27560:2023 specifies an interoperable, open, and extensible information structure for recording a PII principal’s consent to PII processing, supporting: - provision of a record to the PII principal - exchange of consent information between information systems - management of the life cycle of the recorded consent

docs.title: How this repo extends it
docs.text: The 0PN Lab profile lineage in this repository extends ISO/IEC TS 27560:2023 toward Transparency-by-Default infrastructure by adding (at the profile layer): - Controller-ID first disclosure before collection or inference - Bilateral proof-of-notice patterns (receipts held by both controller and individual) - A consolidated “Digital Consent” packaging (DCR v1.2) built on the UNR lineage

docs.title: Recommended reading path (this docs site)
docs.text:
• Start here (DCR v1.2): ../unr/dcr/v1.2/
  • Baseline implementer reference (UNR v1.01): ../unr/v1.01/
  • UNR submission snapshot (v1): ../unr/v1/
  • UNR working draft (v1.1): ../unr/v1.1/

docs.title: OCV — Operational Constraint Vocabulary
docs.text: Supporting vocabulary for expressing operational constraints and interoperability rules

docs.title: What this is
docs.text: OCV provides vocabulary support for expressing constraints that matter operationally (for example: scope, context, applicability, and enforcement-oriented conditions) alongside the profile lineage.

docs.title: How it relates to this repo
docs.text: OCV is a supporting component used to keep: - profile requirements expressible in machine-consumable form - constraints inspectable and testable - implementations consistent across domains and jurisdictions

docs.text:
Code of Conduct Profile packaging built on the UNR profile lineage.

Status: Consolidated draft (v1.2)

Interpretation rule: v1.2 assumes UNR (v1 / v1.01) as prerequisite architecture.

docs.title: What v1.2 is
docs.text:
v1.2 consolidates the UNR lineage into a single packaging framed as Digital Consent (Code of Conduct Profile), centered on:

  • Controller-ID first disclosure before collection or inference
  • Bilateral proof-of-notice (two-party receipts)
  • State tracking and material change visibility (notice event logs)
  • Portability and rights-control patterns where applicable, without surveillance intermediaries

docs.title: UNR v1 — Universal Notice Receipt (Submission Draft)
docs.text:
ISO/IEC 27560 profile contribution (submission draft PDF)

Status: Submission draft (historical anchor) Note: This is a submission artefact and should be treated as non-normative unless explicitly adopted in a formal standards track.

docs.title: Start here
docs.text: • UNR v1 submission draft (PDF): https://github.com/0PN-lab/specs/blob/e19ad403f5ee6a200ff65d02913fd854243b356f/27560%20profile%20/v%201%20-%20UNR%20-%20Notice%20Receipt/27560-1%20Universal%20Notice%20Receipt%20v1%20(Draft%20for%20Submission)141225.pdf

docs.title: Foundation (base standard)
docs.text: • ISO/IEC TS 27560:2023 — Consent record information structure https://www.iso.org/standard/80392.html

docs.title: What this version is
docs.text: UNR v1 establishes the initial profile layer that extends the ISO/IEC TS 27560 base structure into a Transparency-by-Default architecture, centered on: - Controller-ID first disclosure (before collection or inference) - Bilateral notice receipts (proof held by both controller and individual) - A pathway toward notice event logs and progressive assurance levels

docs.title: Recommended reading path
docs.text: If you want the most stable implementer reference, start with: - UNR v1.01 baseline: https://github.com/0PN-lab/specs/blob/e19ad403f5ee6a200ff65d02913fd854243b356f/27560%20profile%20/v%201.01%20-%20UNR%20-%20Notice%20Receipt/ISO-IEC-27560-1-v1.01.md

docs.title: UNR v1.01 — Universal Notice Receipt Profile (Baseline)
docs.text:
Best starting point for stable interpretation and implementer reference

Status: Baseline internal draft (v1.01) Use case: Recommended reference when you need the UNR architecture without draft churn.

docs.title: Start here
docs.text: • UNR v1.01 baseline (Markdown): https://github.com/0PN-lab/specs/blob/e19ad403f5ee6a200ff65d02913fd854243b356f/27560%20profile%20/v%201.01%20-%20UNR%20-%20Notice%20Receipt/ISO-IEC-27560-1-v1.01.md

docs.title: Foundation (base standard)
docs.text: • ISO/IEC TS 27560:2023 — Consent record information structure https://www.iso.org/standard/80392.html

docs.title: What this version is
docs.text: UNR v1.01 is the baseline profile reference that operationalises: - Controller-ID first disclosure - Stage-based exchange (Stage 1 notice receipt as the minimal base pattern) - Bilateral proof-of-notice and auditability primitives that enable Code of Conduct style governance

docs.title: How to use this page
docs.text:
• If you are implementing or reviewing “UNR as prerequisite infrastructure,” use v1.01 as the stable baseline.
  • If you are evaluating enhancements, proceed to v1.1 as a working draft.
  • If you are using the consolidated packaging, proceed to DCR v1.2.

docs.title: ISO-27560 Extension — v1.02
docs.text: This directory contains the v1.02 GitHub-ready Markdown baseline for the ISO/IEC 27560 Universal Notice Receipt Profile extension work.

docs.title: Files
docs.text:
Core specification:

  • README: spec/v1.02/README.md
  • Part 1: spec/v1.02/part-1-co-regulated-architecture.md
  • Part 2: spec/v1.02/part-2-authc-exchange-protocol.md
  • Part 3: spec/v1.02/part-3-delegation-baseline.md

Appendices (split files):

  • Appendix A: spec/v1.02/appendices/appendix-a-field-mapping.md
  • Appendix B: spec/v1.02/appendices/appendix-b-legal-bases-context-implementation.md
  • Appendix C: spec/v1.02/appendices/appendix-c-rights-access-by-context.md

  • Appendix E: spec/v1.02/appendices/appendix-e-open-questions-for-reviewers.md
  • Appendix F: spec/v1.02/appendices/appendix-f-reserved.md

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Appendices I and J are INFORMATIVE and OPTIONAL (do not affect base profile conformance).
  • All internal Notion links have been removed from the GitHub export files.

docs.title: What this version is
docs.text: UNR v1.1 is the working-draft enhancement set that expands the baseline profile toward: - Privacy preference signal handling (for example GPC) - Digital transparency risk assurance levels - Rights automation patterns (where applicable) - Token and exchange extensions (where applicable) - AI transparency extensions (where applicable)

docs.title: How this fits the v1.2 consolidation
docs.text:
DCR v1.2 consolidates UNR v1 + v1.01 + v1.1 into a single “Digital Consent” packaging.

docs.title: Well-known location: notice.txt
docs.text: Controller disclosure entrypoint (Transparency-by-Default pattern)

docs.title: What this page is
docs.text: This page documents the well-known discovery pattern used to publish controller disclosure artefacts (for example a Controller Identification Record and related transparency metadata) at a predictable path on a controller domain.

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Well-known locations enable: - automated discovery by individuals, regulators, and agents - verification before collection (Controller-ID first ordering) - consistent cross-controller interoperability without bespoke integrations

docs.title: Recommended references (repo lineage)
docs.text:
• UNR v1.01 baseline (best starting point): ../../unr/v1.01/
  • DCR v1.2 consolidated packaging: ../../unr/dcr/v1.2/

docs.title: Status
docs.text: This page is a documentation entrypoint. The canonical normative requirements live in the UNR / DCR profile documents referenced above.
